title: Whipple Disease
authors: Thomas A. Russo, Seth R. Glassman

items.title: Understanding the Pathogen: Tropheryma whipplei Characteristics
items.content: Tropheryma whipplei is a gram-positive bacillus with a small genome, indicating its dependency on host or rich environments for survival. Its slow growth rate and genomic variability are crucial in understanding its pathogenicity.
keyPoints: T. whipplei is a weakly staining gram-positive bacillus.
Genome size is less than 1 megabase with incomplete biosynthetic pathways, suggesting host dependency or need for rich environments.
Doubling time of T. whipplei is notably slow at 18 days.
Over 100 genotypes have been identified, all capable of causing similar clinical syndromes.
items.reference: Whipple Disease · Whipple Disease | ETIOLOGIC AGENT

items.title: Diagnostic and Epidemiological Insights from PCR Development
items.content: The advent of PCR-based diagnostics has significantly enhanced the understanding of T. whipplei's epidemiology and clinical presentations, revealing its broader prevalence than previously recognized.
keyPoints: PCR amplification and sequencing confirmed T. whipplei as the causative agent in 1991.
Development of PCR-based diagnostics expanded knowledge on T. whipplei’s epidemiology and clinical syndromes.
Exposure to T. whipplei can lead to asymptomatic carriage, acute disease, or chronic infection (WD).
Chronic infection is rare post-exposure.
items.reference: Whipple Disease · Whipple Disease | ETIOLOGIC AGENT

items.title: Understanding the Epidemiology and Transmission of Whipple Disease (WD)
items.content: Whipple Disease is a rare condition with increased recognition due to PCR-based diagnostics. It has a prevalence of approximately 10 cases per million in the U.S., with seroprevalence indicating significant exposure rates among Western Europeans and rural Africans, likely linked to sanitation differences. The disease shows higher male predominance, particularly in Caucasians, without clear animal or environmental reservoirs. Transmission is suggested through fecal-oral routes, supported by PCR detection in sewage and family members of patients.
keyPoints: Prevalence: ~10 cases per million (U.S.)
Seroprevalence: ~50% Western Europeans, ~75% rural Africans exposed to T. whipplei
Transmission: Fecal-oral route suggested by PCR detection in sewage and family members
items.reference: Whipple Disease · EPIDEMIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY

items.title: Pathogenesis of Whipple Disease: Immune Response and Chronic Carriage
items.content: Whipple Disease involves a complex interplay between host immune response and pathogen modulation. Asymptomatic carriage is common, with chronic infection rates <0.01%. HLA alleles DRB1*13 and DQB1*06 are linked to increased risk, but other factors like IRF4 haploinsufficiency also contribute. Chronic infection features impaired TH1 response, enhanced anti-inflammatory cytokines, M2 macrophage polarization, and blunted T-cell development. Impaired cell-mediated immunity is crucial for chronic carriage, with higher detection rates in HIV-infected individuals.
keyPoints: Asymptomatic carriage: >99% of those exposed
Chronic infection rate: <0.01%
Immune factors: HLA alleles DRB1*13 and DQB1*06, IRF4 haploinsufficiency
items.reference: Whipple Disease · EPIDEMIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY

items.title: Asymptomatic Carriage of T. whipplei
items.content: T. whipplei can be present in asymptomatic individuals, detected via PCR in stool, saliva, duodenal tissue, and rarely blood. The prevalence is higher among those exposed to waste water or sewage.
keyPoints: PCR detection of T. whipplei in stool (1–11%) and saliva (0.2%).
Higher fecal carriage rates in individuals with exposure to waste water/sewage (12–26%) and children in tropical Africa/Asia (20–48%).
Carriage duration can be up to 7 years for the same strain.
Bacterial loads are lighter in asymptomatic carriers compared to active disease.

items.title: Acute Infection with T. whipplei
items.content: T. whipplei can cause acute gastroenteritis and pneumonia, particularly in children and febrile patients in certain regions.
keyPoints: Detected as a cause of acute gastroenteritis in children.
PCR detection in blood of 4.6% of febrile patients (75% <15 years) from rural Senegal vs. 0.25% healthy controls.
Implicated in acute pneumonia, suggesting primary acquisition may lead to pulmonary or intestinal infection.

items.title: Classic Whipple Disease Presentation
items.content: Classic Whipple disease involves a chronic phase with joint symptoms and gastrointestinal manifestations, often misdiagnosed as rheumatologic disorders.
keyPoints: Initial phase: intermittent migratory oligo- or polyarthralgias/seronegative arthritis (knees, wrists, ankles, metacarpal-interphalangeal joints).
Symptoms last 6–8 years before gastrointestinal symptoms appear.
Immunosuppressive therapy can accelerate disease progression; screening for WD may be warranted.
Gastrointestinal symptoms: diarrhea with weight loss, fever, abdominal pain.
Hepatosplenomegaly (10–15%), ascites (10%), anemia, hypereosinophilia.
Common CT finding: mesenteric/retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy.
Endoscopic signs: pale, yellow, or shaggy mucosa with erythema/ulceration past the duodenum.

items.title: Cardiac Disease Manifestations in Whipple Disease
items.content: Endocarditis is increasingly recognized in Whipple disease, particularly affecting males. It accounts for a significant portion of culture-negative endocarditis cases and can lead to severe complications like congestive heart failure and embolic events.
keyPoints: 85% of male cases involve endocarditis
2.6–6.3% of culture-negative endocarditis cases are due to Whipple disease
Complications include congestive heart failure (40%), embolic events, arrhythmias, and mycotic aneurysm
Vegetations identified by echocardiography in 50–75% of cases

items.title: Ocular Disease Presentation in Whipple Disease
items.content: Uveitis is the most common ocular manifestation, often presenting with vision changes or 'floaters'. It can occur in various forms and may be exacerbated by glucocorticoid treatment.
keyPoints: Uveitis is the most common form of ocular disease in Whipple disease
Can present as anterior, intermediate, or posterior uveitis
Glucocorticoids alone can worsen uveitis and unmask extraocular disease

items.title: Dermatologic Signs of Whipple Disease
items.content: Skin hyperpigmentation, particularly in light-exposed areas without adrenal dysfunction, is suggestive of Whipple disease. Other cutaneous manifestations include erythematous macular lesions and subcutaneous nodules.
keyPoints: Melanoderma (skin hyperpigmentation) is suggestive of Whipple disease
Other dermatologic signs: erythematous macular lesions, nonthrombocytopenic purpura, subcutaneous nodules

items.title: Recognizing IRIS in T. whipplei Infection and Its Impact on Clinical Management
items.content: IRIS can occur in up to 17% of patients treated for T. whipplei infection, especially those with prior immunosuppressive therapy. It is characterized by the recurrence of inflammation after initial treatment response and loss of PCR detection.
keyPoints: Up to 17% of patients may experience IRIS post-treatment.
IRIS symptoms include fever, arthritis, skin lesions, subcutaneous nodules, pleuritis, uveitis, orbital and periorbital inflammation.
Some cases can be fatal.

items.title: Diagnostic Approach for T. whipplei Infection: Importance of PCR Testing
items.content: PCR testing is crucial for diagnosing T. whipplei infection, with a cycle threshold value ≤30 in duodenal biopsy specimens aiding diagnosis even when PAS staining is negative.
keyPoints: Serology is not reliable due to poor IgM/IgG response.
Postbulbar duodenal biopsies are recommended despite normal macroscopic appearance.
PCR testing with a cycle threshold value ≤30 can confirm diagnosis in suspected WD cases.

items.title: Utilizing Urine PCR for Noninvasive Diagnosis of T. whipplei Infection
items.content: Urine PCR shows promise as a noninvasive diagnostic tool, with a study showing 75% positivity in cases prior to treatment.
keyPoints: Urine PCR was positive in 75% of cases before treatment in one study.
Controls showed 0% positivity for urine PCR despite fecal PCR positivity in presumed carriers.
Urine PCR can also help evaluate the success of WD therapy.

items.title: Optimal Treatment Regimen for Whipple Disease with CNS Involvement
items.content: For patients with Whipple disease, especially those with CNS involvement, initial treatment should involve intravenous ceftriaxone or meropenem at optimized doses (2 g q12h or 2 g q8h) for 2–4 weeks. This is followed by oral doxycycline or minocycline plus hydroxychloroquine for at least 1 year, if tolerated.
keyPoints: IV ceftriaxone (2 g q12h) or meropenem (2 g q8h) for 2–4 weeks
Follow-up with oral doxycycline or minocycline plus hydroxychloroquine for ≥1 year
TMP-SMX has reported relapses, suggesting caution in critical sites like CNS and heart

items.title: Management of Whipple Disease Endocarditis
items.content: For endocarditis caused by T. whipplei, European guidelines recommend oral doxycycline plus hydroxychloroquine for ≥18 months or IV ceftriaxone (2 g q24h) or penicillin (2 million units q4h) plus streptomycin (1 g q24h) for 2–4 weeks followed by oral TMP-SMX.
keyPoints: Oral doxycycline plus hydroxychloroquine for ≥18 months
IV ceftriaxone (2 g q24h) or penicillin (2 million units q4h) plus streptomycin (1 g q24h) for 2–4 weeks followed by oral TMP-SMX
Treatment durations of 12–13 months reported efficacious in a small study

items.title: Considerations for Lifelong Suppressive Therapy
items.content: Due to the risk of relapse or reinfection, lifelong suppressive therapy with doxycycline is advocated after completing the initial treatment regimen for Whipple disease.
keyPoints: Lifelong suppressive therapy with doxycycline recommended post-treatment
Close follow-up necessary due to potential long-term relapse or reinfection

items.question: Which diagnostic method confirmed the etiologic agent of Whipple disease in 1991?
items.explanation: The hypothesis that Whipple disease is caused by Tropheryma whipplei was confirmed by PCR amplification and sequencing in 1991.
items.reference: Whipple Disease · Whipple Disease | ETIOLOGIC AGENT

items.question: What characteristic of T. whipplei's genome suggests its dependency on a host or rich environment?
items.explanation: T. whipplei has a small (<1-megabase) chromosome with many biosynthetic pathways absent or incomplete, suggesting dependency on a host or rich environment.
items.reference: Whipple Disease · Whipple Disease | ETIOLOGIC AGENT

items.question: What is the doubling time of T. whipplei, making it one of the slowest growing human pathogens?
items.explanation: T. whipplei has a doubling time of 18 days, making it one of the slowest growing human pathogens.
items.reference: Whipple Disease · Whipple Disease | ETIOLOGIC AGENT

items.question: Which population has the highest reported seroprevalence of T. whipplei exposure?
options: 10 cases per million in the U.S.
items.explanation: Seroprevalence studies indicate that ~75% of Africans from rural Senegal have been exposed to T. whipplei.
items.reference: Whipple Disease · EPIDEMIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY

items.question: What is the estimated prevalence of Whipple disease (WD) in the U.S. population?
items.explanation: A recent U.S. epidemiologic survey places the number closer to 10 cases per million.
items.reference: Whipple Disease · EPIDEMIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY

items.question: Which factor is associated with an increased risk of T. whipplei infection?
items.explanation: The HLA alleles DRB1*13 and DQB1*06, which stimulate humoral rather than cell-mediated immune responses, are associated with an increased risk of infection.
items.reference: Whipple Disease · EPIDEMIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY

items.question: Which site has the highest prevalence of T. whipplei carriage in asymptomatic individuals?
items.explanation: The prevalence of fecal carriage (1–11%) is higher than that detected in saliva (0.2%).

items.question: Which group has a significantly elevated prevalence of T. whipplei fecal carriage?
options: Individuals with exposure to waste water or sewage
Individuals without any known risk factors
items.explanation: Prevalence is elevated among individuals with exposure to waste water or sewage (12–26%).

items.question: What is a common initial manifestation of classic Whipple disease?
options: Intermittent, symmetrical migratory oligo- or polyarthralgias
items.explanation: The initial phase of classic Whipple disease often manifests primarily as intermittent, symmetrical migratory oligo- or polyarthralgias.

items.question: Which diagnostic finding is highly suggestive of Whipple disease when observed during endoscopy?
options: Pale, yellow, or shaggy mucosa with erythema or ulceration past the first portion of the duodenum
items.explanation: Pale, yellow, or shaggy mucosa with erythema or ulceration past the first portion of the duodenum suggests Whipple disease.

items.question: Which of the following is a common cardiac manifestation in Whipple disease?
items.explanation: Endocarditis is increasingly recognized in Whipple disease, affecting the aortic and mitral valves.

items.question: In Whipple disease, which form of uveitis is most commonly observed?
items.explanation: Uveitis, particularly anterior uveitis, is the most common form of ocular disease in Whipple disease.

items.question: What imaging finding is commonly associated with Whipple disease lesions?
items.explanation: Lesions in Whipple disease are usually T2 and fluid-attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) hyperintense.

items.question: Which of the following is a dermatologic manifestation suggestive of Whipple disease?
items.explanation: Skin hyperpigmentation (melanoderma) is suggestive of Whipple disease.

items.question: Which of the following is a manifestation of IRIS in patients treated for T. whipplei infection?
options: Development of fever, arthritis, skin lesions, subcutaneous nodules, pleuritis, uveitis, and orbital and periorbital inflammation
Resolution of gastrointestinal symptoms without recurrence
Complete eradication of T. whipplei from all tissues
items.explanation: IRIS in T. whipplei infection can manifest as fever, arthritis, skin lesions, subcutaneous nodules, pleuritis, uveitis, and orbital/periorbital inflammation.

items.question: Which diagnostic procedure remains important for classic Whipple disease but is not optimally sensitive?
options: PCR testing of duodenal biopsy specimens
Histologic findings in intestinal biopsy specimens
items.explanation: Histologic findings in intestinal biopsy specimens are important for diagnosing classic Whipple disease but lack optimal sensitivity.

items.question: What is a limitation of using PAS staining to identify T. whipplei in tissues?
options: It only works on formalin-fixed tissues.
It has high specificity for T. whipplei.
It yields positive results with mycobacteria and other microorganisms as well.
It is more sensitive than PCR.
items.explanation: PAS staining is nonspecific, yielding positive results with mycobacteria and other microorganisms in addition to T. whipplei.

items.question: Which diagnostic tool has greater specificity and sensitivity than PAS staining for identifying T. whipplei?
items.explanation: Immunohistochemistry has greater specificity and sensitivity than PAS staining for identifying T. whipplei.

items.question: Which antibiotic combination has been reported to cure an asymptomatic CNS infection that was not eradicated by other regimens?
items.explanation: The combination of oral minocycline and chloroquine cured an asymptomatic CNS infection that was not eradicated by other regimens.

items.question: What is the recommended treatment for Whipple disease endocarditis with significant valve dysfunction?
options: Oral doxycycline plus hydroxychloroquine for ≥18 months
Ceftriaxone (2 g q24h IV) or penicillin (2 million units q4h IV) plus streptomycin (1 g q24h IV) for 2–4 weeks followed by oral TMP-SMX
items.explanation: The recommended treatment includes ceftriaxone or penicillin plus streptomycin for 2–4 weeks followed by oral TMP-SMX.

items.question: What is the suggested course of action when T. whipplei detection persists in CSF during therapy?
items.explanation: Continued detection of T. whipplei in CSF should prompt continuation or consideration of an alternative therapy.

items.question: What is the current status of a peptide-based vaccine for Whipple disease?
options: It has been approved and widely used
It is in development but may have limited role due to rarity of infection
items.explanation: Attempts are underway to develop a peptide-based vaccine, but its role may be limited due to the rarity of infection.

items.statement: All genotypes of T. whipplei are capable of causing different clinical syndromes.
items.explanation: All genotypes appear to be capable of causing similar clinical syndromes.
items.reference: Whipple Disease · Whipple Disease | ETIOLOGIC AGENT

items.statement: T. whipplei is a gram-negative bacillus.
items.explanation: T. whipplei is a weakly staining gram-positive bacillus.
items.reference: Whipple Disease · Whipple Disease | ETIOLOGIC AGENT

items.statement: T. whipplei has been identified in both sewage water and human feces.
items.explanation: The organism has been identified by PCR in sewage water and human feces.
items.reference: Whipple Disease · EPIDEMIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY

items.statement: WD is more common in females than males.
items.explanation: In most studies, males more commonly develop WD.
items.reference: Whipple Disease · EPIDEMIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY

items.statement: T. whipplei is commonly detected in blood samples from asymptomatic individuals.
items.explanation: Detection of T. whipplei in blood occurs rarely in the absence of symptoms.

items.statement: Neurologic disease in Whipple disease is often asymptomatic.
items.explanation: CNS disease develops in ~50% of patients, many of whom are asymptomatic.

items.statement: Fever is a common symptom in endocarditis associated with Whipple disease.
items.explanation: Fever is often absent in endocarditis cases of Whipple disease.

items.statement: Whipple disease can involve almost any organ system, leading to a multisystemic process.
items.explanation: Whipple disease is characterized by its potential involvement of multiple organs and systems.

items.statement: Serology is highly valuable in diagnosing active T. whipplei infection.
items.explanation: Serology has little value as patients with active infection usually mount a poor IgM/IgG response.

items.statement: Urine PCR can be used noninvasively to diagnose classic and isolated Whipple disease.
items.explanation: Urine PCR holds promise for the noninvasive diagnosis of classic and isolated Whipple disease.

items.statement: A Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction can occur within 24 hours after initiating treatment for Whipple disease.
items.explanation: The occurrence of a Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction within 24 hours of treatment initiation has been described.

items.statement: Lifelong suppressive therapy with doxycycline is not recommended after completing the initial Whipple disease treatment regimen.
items.explanation: Due to the risk of relapse or reinfection, lifelong suppressive therapy with doxycycline has been advocated.

items.assertion: Immunosuppressive therapy can accelerate the progression of Whipple disease.
items.reason: Treatment with immunosuppressants like glucocorticoids or anti-TNF-α can worsen the disease process.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why immunosuppressive therapy should be avoided.

items.assertion: The initial phase of classic Whipple disease is characterized by gastrointestinal symptoms.
items.reason: Gastrointestinal symptoms typically appear before joint symptoms in the disease progression.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the initial phase manifests primarily as intermittent migratory arthralgias, not gastrointestinal symptoms.

items.assertion: The presence of T. whipplei in bronchoalveolar lavage fluid from asymptomatic HIV-infected individuals suggests caution in diagnosing isolated pneumonia.
items.reason: T. whipplei sequence identification does not correlate with increased inflammation or decreased lung function.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Optimal treatment regimens for Whipple disease have been well established.
items.reason: Treatment with oral tetracycline or TMP-SMX alone may result in disease relapse, especially with CNS involvement.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because optimal treatment regimens are still being defined.

items.assertion: PCR testing is essential in confirming the diagnosis of T. whipplei infection.
items.reason: PCR-based diagnostics have significantly increased sensitivity and specificity for identifying T. whipplei.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why PCR testing is essential.

items.assertion: Culturing T. whipplei from blood or CSF is practical for routine diagnosis.
items.reason: T. whipplei can be cultured from various body fluids and tissues, but it takes months to obtain a positive result.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because culturing T. whipplei is not practical due to the long time required; however, the reason is true.

items.assertion: Oral tetracycline is not recommended for CNS Whipple disease due to high relapse rates.
items.reason: Oral tetracycline or TMP-SMX monotherapy had unacceptable relapse rates, especially in CNS disease.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why oral tetracycline is not recommended.

items.assertion: The role of a peptide-based vaccine for Whipple disease is well-established.
items.reason: Attempts to develop a peptide-based vaccine are underway, but its utility may be limited due to the rarity of infection.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the role of the vaccine is not yet established; however, the reason is true.
